posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: held my breath as I opened the bottle and was relieved when the wine poured out light in color, not a trace of the premox that pervades this wine, though it was a bit more honeyed than usual but still had superb flavors and depth. Citrus and some apples, white flowers, not real steely like CC often is, the bottle was emptied rather quickly.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini

posts.cooked: Wonderful, when they are on.

posts.cooked: Opened a 1996 a couple of weeks ago. Originally had a case and have gone through 7 or 8 of them. This was the first one not showing oxidation. I guess I’m an optimist at heart.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked: Opened an '11 Saturday night. No pox. Still coiled upon opening but fanned out with air. Appealing lemon curd flavors. Long and persistent.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked:
Eric Ifune wrote: ↑Mon Feb 08, 2021 3:36 pm
Opened a 1996 a couple of weeks ago. Originally had a case and have gone through 7 or 8 of them. This was the first one not showing oxidation. I guess I’m an optimist at heart.
Alan Weinberg wrote:
need a note!

From memory. It’s not what it was some years ago!

Pale gold, no browning.
Aromas of apples, a bit of toast.
Rich, medium full on the palate. The burnished flavors of maturity. Apples and pears.
Nice length. Very rich on the palate.
This is how the wine should have been.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
I went through a 6 pack of the 2004 starting in 2012, and just drinkin the last in aug…just about every bottle, including the very first, smelled like it was poxed upon pulling the cork. With air, they ALL brightened up, and those baked apple and sherry nuances integrated dramatically, and all bottles were amazing…totally 2004…and enjoyable.
I wonder how many quickly call POX out the gate, and totally miss out on a great wine??

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Hi Reece,

Here is my note on the '84 from 3 year’s ago: A really nice showing. There’s some white mushroom and toasty development on the nose. It has rich white peach fruit and a line of sharp acidity. With food it drinks well.

The BdM’s of the '80’s were particularly good. As William notes, the '85 is still as tight as a drum, as is the '88. The '86 is awesome, quite exotic. I’ve had a couple of splendid bottles of '82. I even had a very serviceable bottle of '87.

Best Regards
Jeremy

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: '17, '18 and '19 may all be very good, but are they worth 3-4 x the price of the 2010? I’m not a buyer.

posts.name: Reece Warren
posts.cooked:
Hi Reece,

Here is my note on the '84 from 3 year’s ago: A really nice showing. There’s some white mushroom and toasty development on the nose. It has rich white peach fruit and a line of sharp acidity. With food it drinks well.

The BdM’s of the '80’s were particularly good. As William notes, the '85 is still as tight as a drum, as is the '88. The '86 is awesome, quite exotic. I’ve had a couple of splendid bottles of '82. I even had a very serviceable bottle of '87.

Best Regards
Jeremy

Thanks Jeremy, most appreciated. Not sure if this has been stored perfectly so may have to open a bottle to check them out.

posts.name: Reece Warren
posts.cooked:
Reece, apologies if I’ve mistaken your identity, but your estimates on Ramonet, including the 2002 Montrachet, are absurd imho. There’s a decent 30-50% chance they are undrinkable, and no right of return. Though I’m sure someone in Auckland who has just made another $500k on their house will probably take the gamble !

Rauno, the Reserves and Estmates on these were set by the vendors. However, they have been stored incrediably well and I would be surprised if the 2002s were out of condition. The other vintages we have again Reserves set by the vendors and have come from cellars that we get a lot of wine from with no issues. Just as a point of interest the 2002 Ramonet Montrachet lowest price in retail is over NZ$5000 (in bond and excluding taxes) so I think our NZ$2000 was pretty reasonable and it sold to a very grateful buyer who told us it was a fantastic bottle of wine.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: I’m glad it was a fantastic bottle, Reece. If one knew beforehand that it’s one of those (fantastic bottles) then it makes the value equation work a lot more easily ! Ramonet haven’t covered themselves in glory re premox, and then there’s the vagaries of storage, provenance etc on top of that. But obviously my opinions and risk tolerance have no bearing on actual pricing for high-end Burgundy at auction, and that’s all fine.
